target: t3-fire-fire-fire (Supernova) vs t2-fire-fire (Pyre)
claim: Supernova is a T3<T2 tier violation / weak detonator.
method: Swap-test, mage, matched burn-banking baselines. (A) boss2 slot4 [t2-agility-fire x4 + t2-attack-fire + t2-earth-fire banking burn]; (B) boss3 slot2; (C) boss3 deep-bank slot6 [6 appliers front-loaded]. Filler=t1-fire, repeats 8-10. Compared Supernova and its T2 sibling Pyre as candidates in the SAME baseline. Also A/B'd a proposed buff.
evidence: boss3 slot2 aggregate Δ vs t1-fire CONTROL: Supernova Δdmg +5.3, ΔttkMs +3167 (SLOWER), Δcushion -0.117; sibling Pyre Δdmg -1.3, ΔttkMs +667. On the lizard_king boss (370HP, unsaturated): Supernova 422dmg/24000ms/hp0.30 vs Pyre 407dmg/21000ms/hp0.37 — Supernova adds only ~15 dmg but is 3000ms slower with worse cushion. On iron_golem: Supernova 421dmg/27500ms vs Pyre 425dmg/26000ms (Pyre better on both). Even in the deep-bank steelman where Supernova posts the biggest aggregate Δdmg (+76, inflated by AoE on single-enemy fights = overkill), on the boss it is 418dmg/22000ms vs Pyre 412dmg/22800ms — a wash. Cost asymmetry: Supernova 2s+1m, CD2.8, EXHAUST (fires once) vs Pyre 1m, CD1.4, no exhaust, repeatable, and Pyre ALSO consumes the burn pool (3/stk) AND re-applies 3 burn each cast.
corrected_statement: Supernova is a genuine tier violation on a value-for-cost basis: at roughly 3x the resource cost and an exhaust tax it fires once for damage comparable to (and often slower/squishier than) the repeatable 1-mana T2 Pyre that does the same consume-pool job. The single-cast peakOPS 28.57 understates it slightly (AoE), but in real single-boss combat it does not out-perform its T2 sibling.
proposed_fix: C1-compliant: it is already elite-priced in BOTH resources, so do NOT add cost. Remove `exhaust:true` and cut cooldown 2.8 -> 2.2 so it can re-fire as a recurring nuke, and raise per-burn damage 5 -> 7 (effects[0].value). Verified A/B (boss3, deep-bank, 10 reps): current Supernova ttk 22000ms -> BUFF (no-exhaust, cd2.2, 7/burn) ttk 19000ms, beating both CONTROL (20000) and Pyre-T2 (22800) without trivializing the boss (cushion stays ~0.45, no deaths).

target: t3-fire-fire-water (Quench Lance) vs t2-fire-water (Steam Surge)
claim: Quench Lance is a T3<T2 tier violation / weak.
method: Swap-test, mage, matched burn baselines (boss2 slot4, boss3 slot2, plus buff A/B at boss3 lizard_king 10 reps). Quench Lance and its T2 sibling Steam Surge placed as candidates in the same baseline; filler t1-fire.
evidence: boss3 slot2 aggregate Δ vs CONTROL: Quench Lance Δdmg +3.3, ΔttkMs +1300 (slower), Δcushion -0.054; sibling Steam Surge Δdmg +7.0, ΔttkMs +333, Δcushion 0.000. Steam Surge beats Quench on damage, TTK AND cushion. Per-boss: iron_golem Quench 421dmg/0.465cush vs Steam 438dmg/0.561cush; lizard_king Quench 410dmg/0.442 vs Steam 423dmg/0.465. Quench Lance is strictly dominated by its own T2 sibling on every axis. Structural cause: Quench (1s+1m, CD2.4) only applies 2 burn + a conditional int-gain aura gated on consuming 10+ burn (rarely triggers); Steam Surge (1m, CD1.3) deals 6+4 damage, heals 4, AND grants 1 stamina. peakOPS confirms: Quench 8.33 vs Steam 19.23.
corrected_statement: Quench Lance is a clear tier violation: a T3 that is strictly worse than its own T2 sibling Steam Surge in damage, kill-speed, and survivability, while its only T3 hook (int-gain on consuming 10+ burn) almost never fires. It is the weakest fire card tested and a prime unused-tier candidate.
proposed_fix: C1-compliant buff of the T3 (never nerf Steam Surge): cut cooldown 2.4 -> 1.6, raise burn applied 2 -> 4 (effects[0].value), lower the int-gain threshold from 10 -> 6 burn consumed (effects[1].event_counter.filter.min_amount), and add a modest immediate hit (~6 int-scaled damage) so it is a viable opener rather than a dead enabler. Verified A/B (boss3 lizard_king, 10 reps): Quench-current ttk 21000ms/cush 0.494 -> Quench-BUFF ttk 20000ms/cush 0.564, now matching Steam Surge (20000ms/0.587) without becoming oppressive.

target: t3-attack-fire-fire (Cinderlance) vs t2-fire-fire (Pyre) / t2-agility-fire (Flame Dart)
claim: Cinderlance is a T3<T2 tier violation / weak.
method: Swap-test, mage, matched burn baselines (boss2 slot4, boss3 slot2, deep-bank slot6). Cinderlance vs T2 fire siblings as candidates in the same banking baseline; filler t1-fire.
evidence: boss3 slot2 aggregate Δ vs CONTROL: Cinderlance Δdmg +16.3 — clearly above both T2 fire siblings (Pyre -1.3, Flame Dart -0.3). On lizard_king it posts 461dmg (highest single-target damage after Cinder Sprint), well above Pyre's 407 and Flame Dart's 409. peakOPS 38.18 > both T2 siblings.
corrected_statement: Cinderlance is NOT a tier violation — its damage delta comfortably exceeds every T2 fire sibling in a burn-banking deck (8 str-scaled x2 hits PLUS a 3-Pierce-per-burn detonation). Its weaknesses are slow TTK (+2667ms) and poor cushion (-0.101) from the 2.2s CD, but its ceiling is genuinely T3-grade. No buff needed for tier-compliance; if anything its CD2.2 could drop to ~1.8 to improve cadence, but that is polish, not a violation fix.

target: t3-agility-fire-fire (Cinder Sprint) vs t2-agility-fire (Flame Dart)
claim: Cinder Sprint is a T3<T2 tier violation / weak.
method: Swap-test, mage, matched burn baselines (boss2 slot4, boss3 slot2, deep-bank slot6). Cinder Sprint vs its direct T2 sibling Flame Dart as candidates in the same banking baseline; filler t1-fire.
evidence: boss3 slot2 aggregate Δ vs CONTROL: Cinder Sprint Δdmg +30.3 — the highest damage delta of ALL fire candidates and far above its T2 sibling Flame Dart (Δdmg -0.3). On lizard_king it tops the field at 495dmg vs Flame Dart's 409 (+86). It is also the cheapest T3 here (1m only, CD1.6) and self-feeds (applies 3 burn then immediately detonates the whole pool at 4/dex per stack).
corrected_statement: Cinder Sprint is NOT a tier violation — it is the strongest fire T3 by isolated damage and decisively beats its T2 sibling Flame Dart on every banking baseline. Its only cost is a slightly slower TTK and lower cushion than the filler-overkill control on the boss (the detonation spends the pool then leaves it empty), but its damage ceiling is the highest in the lane. Leave as-is.

target: t3-air-fire-fire (Pyre Surge) vs t2-fire-fire (Pyre)
claim: Pyre Surge is a T3<T2 tier violation / weak (peakOPS only 13.33).
method: Swap-test, mage, matched burn baselines (boss2 slot4, boss3 slot2, deep-bank slot6). Pyre Surge vs T2 sibling Pyre as candidates in the same banking baseline; filler t1-fire.
evidence: boss3 slot2 aggregate Δ vs CONTROL: Pyre Surge Δdmg +19.3 AND ΔttkMs -367 (the ONLY candidate that speeds up kills) with the smallest cushion loss (-0.015). On lizard_king: Pyre Surge 453dmg/17000ms/hp0.512 vs Pyre 407dmg/22800ms/hp0.424 — wins damage, TTK (5800ms faster), AND cushion. On iron_golem it is the single best card: 469dmg/20000ms vs CONTROL 438/22000. Single-cast peakOPS (13.33) badly understates it because it doubles the WHOLE banked pool and adds +2 burn-taken for 8s — a multiplier on the rest of the deck's burn, exactly the Tempest-Cadence-style whole-deck accelerator that OPS cannot see.
corrected_statement: Pyre Surge is NOT a tier violation and is in fact the best-performing fire card in a burn-banking deck. Its low peakOPS (13.33) is a classic OPS blind spot: as a burn doubler + damage-taken amplifier its value is multiplicative on the rest of the deck's banked burn (analogous to Tempest Cadence's whole-deck Haste), which single-cast OPS cannot measure. No buff needed.

target: t3-air-air-air (Tempest Cadence) — claimed T3>T2 tier violation vs t2-air-air (Tailwind)
claim: Tempest Cadence is a tier violation: its isolated OPS (24.17) is no better than a T2, so the T3 fails to beat T2 air cards.
method: swap-test, mage, candidate in slot 0 of matched thin AIR-tempo baselines, both T3 and its T2 sibling as candidates in the SAME deck/slot. boss1/doom_knight (380hp) 10 reps; boss6/infernal_dragon (460hp) 12 reps; loop25 single-target (werewolf+baby_dragon) 10 reps. Files: tests/audit/val/swap/air-thin*.json, air-stress*.json, air-aoe*.json.
evidence: All matchups winRate 1.0 (de-saturated on TTK/cushion). boss6: Tempest cush 0.198 / ttk 21000 / 31 cards vs Tailwind cush 0.278 / ttk 19000 / 33 cards vs CONTROL(t1-air) cush 0.198 / ttk 21000 / 36 cards. loop25 werewolf: Tempest ttk 11000 (Δ-1000) cush 0.767; Tailwind ttk 10700 (Δ-1300) cush 0.814. Tempest needs ~5 fewer cards to close than the filler (more damage density) and is at PARITY with its T2 sibling on single-target TTK/cushion — it is not below it. Its structural T3 edge (AoE 'to all enemies' + Haste 30%/10s vs Tailwind's single-target + 20%/5s) accelerates the whole deck and pays off in multi-enemy waves the single-enemy harness cannot measure (matches the prior poison-baseline REFUTAL).
corrected_statement: Tempest Cadence is NOT a tier violation. In a matched air-tempo baseline it ties its T2 sibling Tailwind on isolated single-target TTK/cushion and closes fights with fewer cards; OPS undersells it because its long whole-deck Haste and all-enemies AoE are deck-level/multi-target effects OPS and single-enemy swaps don't capture. The T3 edge is a higher ceiling when assembled (AoE + sustained Haste), exactly per the tier charter.
proposed_fix: No change required (C4: wiring is correct). If the team wants a clearer T3 felt-power margin over Tailwind on single targets, the C1-compliant lever is a small CD cut (2.4s -> 2.0s) so the AoE/Haste fires more often; do NOT raise cost or nerf Tailwind.

target: t3-agility-air-air (Gale Echo)
claim: Gale Echo is a viable T3 air/agility card (slow-amplifier aura + Haste).
method: Same air swap-tests (slot 0, matched air baseline). boss1/doom_knight 10 reps and boss6/infernal_dragon 12 reps. Gale Echo carried as a candidate alongside Tempest, Tailwind, Gale Cut. Files: air-thin*.json, air-stress*.json.
evidence: Gale Echo was the WORST card in every air test. boss1: ttk 36000 / cush 0.378 vs the CONTROL filler's ttk 32000 / cush 0.606 (it made the deck SLOWER and squishier than playing nothing). boss6: ttk 24000 / cush 0.118, worst of all five variants (CONTROL ttk 21000 / cush 0.198; Tailwind ttk 19000 / cush 0.278). peakOPS 0. Its payoff ('every time you apply [slow], apply 1 more [slow]') is dead unless the deck is already spamming slow, and slow is C5 pure soft control (no damage) — so against a single boss it contributes zero damage and zero survival while occupying the slot.
corrected_statement: Gale Echo is an off-curve WEAK T3: a slow-amplifier aura that does literally nothing (negative marginal value) outside a dedicated slow/stun control shell, dragging TTK and cushion below an empty filler slot against bosses. It is a likely unused-tier card.
proposed_fix: C1+C4: give it a baseline body so it is not dead off-theme. Cut CD 2.4s -> 1.6s and add a flat hit, e.g. 'Deal 4([dex]) to all enemies. Apply 3[slow]([int]). For 12s, every time you apply [slow], apply 1 more [slow]([int]). Haste 15% for 6s.' That seeds its own slow stacks (so the aura self-enables) and gives a damage floor, while staying C5 (slow stays pure control; the only damage is the upfront hit). Keep cost 1s+1m.

target: t3-water-water-water (Tidesong Aura) vs t2-water-water (Frostbind) — T3>T2 tier check
claim: Tidesong Aura (T3 water heal) is a strong T3, clearly better than the T2 water card Frostbind at the water archetype's defensive role.
method: swap-test, mage, matched POISON/HEAL baseline, candidate in slot 4 replacing a t1-water (Mend) filler; Tidesong, Frostbind, and Slipvenom (strong-T3 reference) all candidates in the SAME slot/deck. boss1/doom_knight 10 reps and boss6/infernal_dragon 12 reps. Files: tests/audit/val/swap/water-thin*.json, water-stress*.json.
evidence: Tidesong is OUT-SURVIVED by its own T2 sibling at the defensive job. boss1: Frostbind cush 0.811 / ttk 33000 vs Tidesong cush 0.803 / ttk 36000 (Frostbind matches healing AND kills faster). boss6 (decisive, hard-hitting 9-dmg boss): Frostbind cush 0.873 vs Tidesong cush 0.406 — MORE THAN DOUBLE — and Frostbind ttk 34000 vs Tidesong 37000. Frostbind's 1[stun]+armor freezes the boss's attack timer (C5: stun = hard control), preventing damage outright, which beats raw healing. Meanwhile Slipvenom (the strong-T3 reference) closed at ttk 21000 (16s faster) with 14 cards. Tidesong costs 2 mana / 2.4s CD; Frostbind costs only 1 mana / 1.4s CD. So the T3 is strictly more expensive, longer-CD, AND weaker at survival than its T2 sibling.
corrected_statement: Tidesong Aura is a T3>T2 violation at its own (defensive/sustain) role: the T2 Frostbind delivers equal-or-better HP cushion (0.873 vs 0.406 at boss6) and faster TTK for half the mana and a much shorter CD, because stun prevents damage more efficiently than healing absorbs it. Tidesong's only edge is raw heal volume + spi-scaling, which is redundant when the fight is already won. Per the brief this must be fixed by BUFFING the T3, never nerfing Frostbind.
proposed_fix: C1: cut Tidesong CD 2.4s -> 1.6s so it can out-pace incoming boss damage (sustain wants high uptime), keep cost 2m. C2/C3: lean into its scaling identity without armor->damage conversion — e.g. add a control rider so it does the survival job a T3 should: 'Heal 18([spi]). Gain 2[spi] this combat (max 4). Gain 3[mana]. If below 50%[HP]: apply 1[stun]([int]).' This makes the T3 strictly beat Frostbind's stun-when-low while staying within C3 (defense buys time; no heal->damage).

target: t3-agility-water-water (Slipvenom Tempo) — strong-T3 reference sanity check
claim: Slipvenom Tempo is a healthy, well-tuned T3 poison/tempo card (used as the strong-T3 reference).
method: Same water swap-tests (slot 4, matched poison/heal baseline). boss1/doom_knight 10 reps and boss6/infernal_dragon 12 reps. Files: water-thin*.json, water-stress*.json.
evidence: Slipvenom was the clear marginal-value winner on the metric that ends fights. boss1: ttk 22000 vs CONTROL 34000 (-12000) and vs Tidesong 36000 / Frostbind 33000, using only 14 cards (vs 19). boss6: ttk 21000 vs CONTROL 36000 (-15000) with 14 cards and a healthy cush 0.524. It applies 5[poison] (10 with the >=10 ramp) + 15% Haste for 1s+1m, so it both ramps the poison win-con quadratically (C5) and accelerates the deck. No winRate loss, no over-correction signal (cushion stays mid, not trivializing).
corrected_statement: Slipvenom Tempo is correctly tuned and is a valid strong-T3 reference: it dominates marginal TTK in a poison shell (~15s faster kills than filler at both boss1 and boss6) without being oppressive. It is the model a fixed Gale Echo / Tidesong should aspire to.
proposed_fix: No change. Use as the calibration anchor for buffing the weak T3s (Gale Echo, Tidesong) — they should reach a comparable marginal-value band in their own archetype shell.

target: t1-counter (Riposte) — T1 physical counter
claim: Riposte (t1-counter) is off-curve weak vs the t1-attack (Jab) filler in a warrior physical deck.
method: Swap-test, warrior boss1, candidate in slot 0 and slot 2, matched physical baselines, 8-10 repeats vs skeleton/fire_elemental/doom_knight. Also a Riposte-FAVORABLE all-fast-attack baseline (max aura uptime) to give it best case.
evidence: Spec A (slot0) avgΔdmg -13 / avgΔttk +2400 vs Jab. Spec B (slot2, enabled) avgΔdmg -4.0 / avgΔttk +3067 (slowest of all 5 candidates). In the Riposte-FAVORABLE all-fast-attack deck it was STILL strictly worse than the Jab filler: skeleton Δdmg -26, fire_elemental Δdmg -30, doom_knight Δdmg -68 (both decks died at the boss, Riposte dealt 240 vs Jab 308 before dying). peakOPS index = 0. Every metric, every enemy, every baseline: Riposte <= Jab.
corrected_statement: Riposte is a genuine unused-tier T1: its 'for 6s, every hit deal 3' aura (cd 2.0s, 1 stamina) yields negative marginal value vs the plain 9-damage Jab (cd 1.2s) even in the high-attack-frequency deck that should favor it. The 6s window plus 2.0s cd means it spends a card slot on an aura that re-arms slower than Jab just hits, and the 3-per-hit is unconditioned-but-tiny. It is a flat downgrade and will go unused.
proposed_fix: C1 (rescue weak short-window card by cutting CD + lifting value, cost is the lever not CD/tier): cut cooldown 2.0s -> 1.2s so the aura has ~100% uptime, and raise on-hit value 3 -> 5 (and/or extend window 6s -> 8s). Keep cost 1 stamina. Target parity with Jab's ~24 peakOPS when the hero is attacking each tick; do NOT add a second effect (T1 stays clean single-effect per the tier charter).

target: t3-attack-counter-counter (Cleaver's Tax) — overload_lockout_ms is a DEAD field (wiring bug)
claim: Cleaver's Tax has a dead-CD-downside bug: its printed '...and this card delays 4 more seconds next time' never fires, so the card is stronger than its text.
method: Static trace of CardResolver.ts (overload_lockout_ms grep) + in-sim A/B: as-shipped vs a version that carries the SAME 4s as a real conditional cd_debt effect; plus an unconditional-cd_debt sanity probe to prove the cd_debt mechanic itself works; 12 repeats vs doom_knight.
evidence: overload_lockout_ms is referenced ONLY in CardText.ts (prose) and types.ts; CardResolver applies cooldown debt EXCLUSIVELY in 'case cd_debt:' (line 786-790). Cleaver's Tax carries overload_lockout_ms:4000 as a FIELD on a damage effect (cards.json effects[1]), which no resolver branch reads. A/B proof: as-shipped vs adding a real rage-gated cd_debt(4) gave IDENTICAL results (wr1, dmg409, ttk20100, cush0.777, cards15). Sanity probe: an UNCONDITIONAL cd_debt(6) DID slow the same deck ttk 20100 -> 26100 (+6000ms), proving the mechanic works and the as-shipped field is simply never honored. Separately, the rage +20 Pierce bonus DOES fire (threshold-5 reachable: bonus-on ttk 20100/cush .777 vs bonus-off-thr999 ttk 22900/cush .726).
corrected_statement: The 4s self-lockout is purely cosmetic text. The engine grants Cleaver's Tax the full 12 + (rage>=5) 20 Pierce burst on a flat 2.0s cd with NO penalty. A faithfully-implemented 4s lockout would cost the deck roughly +4000ms ttk per use (interpolating the 6s probe), so the card is materially stronger than advertised — an off-curve-STRONG that is currently masked because it reads as self-punishing.
proposed_fix: Wiring fix (C4 wiring-first): either (a) implement overload_lockout_ms as an honored field on any effect (read it in CardResolver alongside the damage resolve and push to cooldownDebtSec, gated by the same condition), OR (b) re-author the card to express the downside as a real cd_debt effect (type:'cd_debt', value:4, target:'self', condition:{self_stack_atleast:{stack:'rage',value:5}}). Prefer (a) so the dozens of cards using the field via text get the mechanic. After the fix, re-check it still kills doom_knight fastest (it should, the penalty only lands AFTER the burst).

target: t3-attack-counter-counter (Cleaver's Tax) vs T2 siblings — suspected tier violation
claim: Cleaver's Tax (T3) is a tier violation because its isolated Δdamage (+8.3) is below its T2 physical siblings Razor Stance (+16.0) and Shield Bash (+12.7) in the same matched baseline.
method: Matched-archetype swap-test putting the T3 and both candidate T2s in the SAME enabled warrior baseline (rage + armor enablers in slots 0-1, candidate slot 2), boss1, 8 repeats vs skeleton/fire_elemental/doom_knight. Judged on kill-speed (ΔttkMs) not just total-damage, per the Tempest-Cadence integrity lesson.
evidence: All matchups win at wr 1.0. By raw avgΔdmg, T3 Cleaver (+8.3) trails Razor Stance (+16.0) and Shield Bash (+12.7) — the apparent 'violation'. But by avgΔttkMs, Cleaver's Tax is the ONLY candidate that kills FASTER than the Jab control (-267ms) while both T2s are SLOWER (Razor +2700, Shield Bash +2000). At the actual boss (doom_knight, def 3): Cleaver Δttk -2400ms (fastest of all 5), Spec A boss Δdmg +71. The Pierce burst closes the armored boss faster; the T2 bleed-aura / armor-scaling pile up DoT and overkill damage AFTER the relevant kill window, inflating avgDamageDealt without speeding the kill. Δdamage is the misleading proxy here exactly like the refuted Tempest Cadence case.
corrected_statement: There is NO tier violation when the cards are judged by their real job (killing the boss). Cleaver's Tax kills doom_knight fastest of any candidate (Δttk -2400ms / -267ms avg) and posts the largest single-card boss damage swing (+71 in Spec A). It only 'loses' on total-damage-dealt, a proxy that over-credits the T2s' post-kill bleed/overkill. The T3's higher ceiling vs the armored target is intact (Pierce is its build-around edge). No buff is needed on damage grounds.
proposed_fix: No tier-violation buff. The only change Cleaver's Tax needs is the overload wiring fix (separate finding) which makes it FAIRER, not weaker, at the burst. Do NOT nerf Razor Stance/Shield Bash; do NOT add power to Cleaver. Per C1/tier-charter, T3 edge = higher ceiling when assembled (Pierce vs armor), which is confirmed.

target: t2-counter-counter (Razor Stance) — suspected weak
claim: Razor Stance (T2 counter-counter) is a suspected weak off-curve T2.
method: Matched warrior physical baseline (DEX/bleed has hit-frequency support), boss1, candidate slot 0 and slot 2, 8 repeats vs skeleton/fire_elemental/doom_knight.
evidence: Spec B avgΔdmg +16.0 vs Jab filler (the BEST of the two T2s tested and above the T3 Cleaver's raw-damage figure). Per-enemy: fire_elemental Δdmg +21, doom_knight Δdmg +27. Positive marginal value on every enemy. Its 'every hit apply 1 bleed (dex), Vengeance +4s' aura compounds with the fast-attack baseline. (Its ΔttkMs is positive/slower because bleed is a DoT that finishes after the burst window — expected for a DoT card, not a weakness.)
corrected_statement: Razor Stance is NOT weak — it is a positive-value T2 bleed enabler with the highest raw-damage delta of the T2s tested. Its only 'negative' metric (slower ttk) is the inherent ramp of a DoT/aura, which is its design. No buff warranted.
proposed_fix: No change. If anything it is a healthy reference point for the weak T1 Riposte (which shares the counter element and on-hit-aura pattern but pays off far worse).

target: t2-attack-defense (Shield Bash) and t3-attack-defense-earth (Earthcleaver) — scanned low-OPS physical
claim: Shield Bash and Earthcleaver are suspected low-OPS off-curve physical cards.
method: Same matched warrior swap-tests (armor enabler t1-defense/Guard +12 armor present in slots 0-1 so both armor-conditionals are live), boss1, 8 repeats vs skeleton/fire_elemental/doom_knight.
evidence: Earthcleaver (T3): Spec B avgΔdmg +22.3 (HIGHEST of all 5 candidates), boss Δdmg +27-28, avgΔttk only +400 — clearly fine/strong, no action. Shield Bash (T2 'deal damage = your armor'): avgΔdmg +12.7 but highly armor-dependent and swingy — skeleton Δdmg +25, fire_elemental +15, but doom_knight Δdmg -2 (the boss out-races armor accrual, so the card stalls exactly when you need it). It only works with an armor-generating partner in front of it (without Guard it does ~0).
corrected_statement: Earthcleaver is healthy (top damage delta), no change. Shield Bash is NOT broadly weak but is conditionally weak at the boss: it converts armor->damage (an allowed narrow line under C3) yet its output collapses against a high-pressure boss that prevents armor from accumulating, so its marginal value at the headline doom_knight fight is ~zero. It is a fine normal-clear card, a poor boss card.
proposed_fix: Earthcleaver: none. Shield Bash (optional, C1): leave cost/CD; consider a small floor so it is not dead when armor is low (e.g. 'deal damage equal to your armor, minimum 6') so it stays relevant in armor-denied boss fights without raising its ceiling — keeps it a snappy 1.6s/1-stamina T2 and does not violate C3 (still armor-sourced, just floored).

target: mush (normal enemy, earth affinity, 122 HP / 3 dmg / 0 def)
claim: The 'mush soft-lock' flagged in the prior pass is a real difficulty problem that persists under the corrected random-tier matrix.
method: Re-aggregated tests/audit/val/results-v2.json (CORRECTED matrix, 1718 normal matchup rows). Mush is the ONLY normal whose mean win rate is dragged down by genuine DEATHS (not just timeouts): deathRate 0.091, timeoutRate 0.045, meanWinRate 0.864, minWinRate 0. Split by class: warrior absWinRate 0.750 (3 of 12 matchups lose), mage absWinRate 1.000 (10/10). Read src/systems/combat/EnemyAffinity.ts: earth affinity on EVERY enemy attack drains 2 stamina from the hero AND sprouts +1 enemy armor (ratchets to cap 15). Mush combines HIGH HP (122) + LOW damage (3), so fights drag long; over a long fight the 2-stamina/hit drain starves a thin warrior deck of resources to play cards while armor ratchets to 15 and blunts low physical hits. Loser rows: war-pure-physical-loop2-1 (2 deaths, 116/122 dmg over 49 cards), early-starters-loop2-2/starter-haste (2 deaths, 92 dmg over 215 cards, 169 reshuffles), rand-loop2-warrior-1 (2 timeouts, only 49 dmg over 323 cards/268 reshuffles). Winning warrior decks killed mush in 7-22 cards (burst before armor/stamina pressure mattered).
evidence: results-v2.json: mush meanWinRate 0.864, deathRate 0.091, timeoutRate 0.045, minWinRate 0 (enemy-summary.json lines 14-20). All 5 normal-enemy sub-100% rows are WARRIOR at loopMult=1.0; mush owns 3 of them (2 with clean deaths). Warrior-vs-mush absWr 0.750 vs mage-vs-mush absWr 1.000. EnemyAffinity.ts earth case: heroStamina -= 2, enemyDefense += 1 (cap 15) per hit. forge_slime (fire, 131 HP, 3 dmg, 2 def — same low damage but NOT earth) = 100% win 0 deaths, isolating earth-affinity as the cause.
corrected_statement: The mush soft-lock is CONFIRMED but NARROW and class-specific: it only afflicts WARRIORS running thin / low-burst decks (all 3 losses are 5-card loop2 warrior decks; mages win 100%). The mechanism is earth-affinity stamina drain (2/hit) starving the warrior of resources combined with the armor ratchet (to 15) blunting low hits in a long fight enabled by mush's unusually high HP-to-damage ratio (122 HP vs 3 dmg). It is not a broad difficulty problem — 16/19 normals are perfectly trivial (100% win across all quality tiers).
proposed_fix: Cut mush baseHP from 122 to ~95-100 (in line with other earth/low-damage normals like ancient_tree 111 HP / depths_horror 95 HP). The fix targets the ROOT: mush's only soft-lock lever is fight LENGTH (122 HP at 3 dmg = long grind that lets earth stamina-drain + armor ratchet dominate). Shortening the fight lets thin warrior decks close before resources are exhausted, without buffing player cards (C4: this is an enemy-tuning fix, no card touch needed). Do NOT raise mush damage (would over-correct a loop-2 starter normal into a threat). Alternatively/additionally, cap the earth-affinity stamina drain per fight or floor warrior stamina regen so a starved warrior always retains minimum tempo — but the HP cut is the simplest, lowest-risk fix.

target: giant_spider (normal enemy, water affinity, 144 HP / 3 dmg / 0 def)
claim: giant_spider is too hard for early decks (it appears in the sub-100% normal set).
method: results-v2.json: giant_spider meanWinRate 0.955, deathRate 0, timeoutRate 0.045, minWinRate 0. The single sub-100% row (rand-loop2-warrior-1) is a TIMEOUT, not a death: avgDamageDealt 263 to a 144 HP enemy over 287 cards played / 209 reshuffles. Water affinity (EnemyAffinity.ts) heals the enemy +4 HP per hit; a low-burst random 5-card warrior deck dealt 263 cumulative damage but could not out-race the +4/hit heal, so the fight timed out. Zero deaths in all 22 matchups.
evidence: enemy-summary.json giant_spider: meanWinRate 0.955, deathRate 0, timeoutRate 0.045. The one failure row: timeouts=2, deaths=0, avgDamageDealt 263 (>144 HP) — a heal-race stall, not the enemy dealing lethal damage (3 dmg is harmless). forge_slime/giant_spider_2 (same low-damage tier) win 100%.
corrected_statement: giant_spider is NOT too hard — it deals 0 lethal pressure (3 dmg, 0 deaths ever). Its single failure is a water-affinity HEAL-RACE timeout against one pathologically weak random 5-card deck that lacked any damage win-condition (263 chip damage couldn't beat +4/hit healing). This is a weak-deck / no-win-condition artifact, not enemy over-tuning, and is far less severe than mush (no deaths).
proposed_fix: No enemy change required. If desired for polish, this is the same family as the mush long-fight problem: giant_spider's 144 HP is the highest of any normal while dealing only 3 damage, so it invites grind stalls. Optionally trim baseHP 144->~120 to shorten the heal-race window. Lowest priority — single timeout, zero deaths.

target: mutated_salamander (normal enemy, water affinity, 160 HP / 8 dmg / 0 def)
claim: mutated_salamander can kill the player (it shows a death in the matrix).
method: results-v2.json: across 187 matchups (374 repeats) mutated_salamander has meanWinRate 0.995, deathRate 0.005 (exactly 2 deaths in 374 sims, a single matchup: rand-loop5-warrior-2, an 8-card random deck). Every authored/archetype deck beats it 100%. 1 losing matchup of 187.
evidence: enemy-summary.json mutated_salamander: nMatchups 187, meanWinRate 0.995, deathRate 0.005, timeoutRate 0, minWinRate 0 driven by ONE random 8-card deck. winRateByArchetype shows only random/warrior at 0.947; all 40+ other archetypes = 1.0.
corrected_statement: mutated_salamander is NOT a tuning problem. Its single death (deathRate 0.005, 1 of 187 matchups) is a lone weak random-deck RNG outlier, statistically indistinguishable from noise. It is its highest-damage normal (8 dmg) so it provides healthy early pressure (mean cushion 0.846) without being unfair.
proposed_fix: No change. This is correctly tuned (provides the most HP pressure of any normal while still winning 99.5% of the time) — exactly the 'beatable with some cost' profile the brief wants for a mid-stage normal.

target: Normal-enemy roster overall (are any normals trivially easy and in need of buffing?)
claim: Some normals are trivially easy across quality tiers and need buffing.
method: quality-matrix.json shows normalWinRate is 0.988-1.000 in EVERY band (1-10 through 61-70) across random/naive/optimized/mixed quality tiers. enemy-summary.json: 16/19 normals have meanWinRate 1.0. Cushion analysis (results-v2.json): worst-case single-matchup cushion floors are corpse_eater 0.214 and lava_golen 0.329 — so SOME normals CAN bite a fragile deck — but mean cushions sit 0.74-0.99, and lost_lizard (68 HP, 2 dmg) is essentially free (cushion 0.988).
evidence: quality-matrix.json normalWinRate by band: 1-10 random 0.988 / optimized 1.0; 11-20 through 61-70 all 1.0. enemy-summary.json: only mush/giant_spider/mutated_salamander dip below 1.0; the other 16 normals are 1.0 mean win across all archetypes and quality tiers. Cushion floors: corpse_eater min 0.214, lava_golen min 0.329 (these DO pressure weak decks).
corrected_statement: Most normals ARE trivially won (16/19 at 100%), but per the WORKFLOW-BRIEF this is BY DESIGN — normals should be 'winRate 1.0 with comfortable cushion'; only the first BOSS (doom_knight) is the 'should be beatable but not trivial' check. Normals are not supposed to threaten. No normal needs a difficulty BUFF. The only outlier worth tuning is mush (the lone normal that produces real deaths), and that is a fix to make it LESS punishing for warriors, not more.
proposed_fix: No buffs to any normal. Per brief, trivially-won normals are the intended baseline; difficulty lives in bosses. Concentrate normal-enemy tuning effort solely on the mush warrior soft-lock (HP cut, see mush finding). Leave the other 18 normals unchanged.

target: boss-curve-flatness (whole rotation, doom_knight loop10 → boss_iron_golem loop70)
claim: The boss difficulty curve is too flat — random/naive decks clear deep bosses comfortably; the curve is not just flat but INVERTED in cushion.
method: Re-read boss-report.json + enemy-summary.json (corrected 2045-matchup results-v2 data). Compared meanWinRate / meanCushionOnWin / fracDecksAnyLoss across all 7 bosses against their scaled HP/dmg. Ran a confirming enemyOverride sim (boss-calib-results.json): a realistic loop-10 naive warrior deck vs doom_knight at 380/6 (base) vs raised HP/dmg.
evidence: Per-boss meanWR/cushion/anyLoss: doom_knight(loop10,scaled 380/6) 0.974/0.595/2.6%; iron_golem(20,420/10) 0.932/0.795/6.8%; lizard_king(30,407/11) 0.966/0.795/3.4%; bog_witch(40,471/8) 1.000/0.909/0%; desert_golem(50,540/12) 0.956/0.836/4.4%; infernal_dragon(60,575/11) 0.895/0.607/10.5%; boss_iron_golem(70,611/15) 0.977/0.747/2.3%. The LAST boss has HIGHER winRate (0.977 vs 0.974) and HIGHER cushion (0.747 vs 0.595) than the FIRST. bog_witch at loop 40 is the SAFEST fight in the whole game (100% WR, 0.909 cushion). Confirming sim: base doom_knight (380HP/6dmg) gives naive a 0.66 cushion at WR 1.0 — never threatened. Cushion never trends downward with loop depth.
corrected_statement: CONFIRMED and stronger than originally stated: the curve isn't merely flat, it is inverted on cushion — bosses 1, 6 are the only ones that bite, and even boss 1 only via a thin cushion, never a loss for a competent deck. Headline failure: doom_knight at base 380HP/6dmg cannot pressure even a naive loop-10 deck (cushion 0.66, 0 deaths). The dominant lever is the multi_hit behavior + raw damage, NOT HP. Difficulty does not increase monotonically with rotation index.
proposed_fix: C1-compliant (enemy tuning, not card nerfs): re-grade the rotation so scaled stats increase monotonically AND so each boss's PER-ATTACK damage (the real lever) scales. Concretely raise base damage across the rotation (doom_knight 6→10 is the single biggest fix) and give every mid/late boss either multi_hit≥2 at damageMultiplier≥0.6 or enrage that actually fires; demote bog_witch's safety by adding a real multi_hit (see its finding). Target: monotone scaled-HP and scaled-damage by loop, with infernal_dragon-class multi_hit pressure on every boss from loop 40+.

target: doom_knight (first boss, loop 10, 380HP / def3 / 6dmg / cd2.5s, enrage@0.5)
claim: doom_knight 'should be EASY but beatable with cost' — the realism headline. Audit said MOSTLY PASS at 97.4% WR / 0.595 cushion.
method: Confirming enemyOverride sweep (boss-calib-results.json, boss-gradient2-results.json) of a realistic loop-10 naive warrior deck (runXP 560), a weak-random deck, and an aggro 'optimized' deck vs doom_knight at base and at raised HP/damage, 8 repeats each.
evidence: Base 380/6: naive WR 1.0 cush 0.66; weak-random WR 0 (6/6 deaths) — so a TRULY weak deck already loses, the boss isn't free for everyone. But the damage lever is mis-set: at base HP 380 with damage 6→12, naive still WINS (WR 1.0) but cushion collapses 0.66→0.23 — damage, not HP, controls the fight. Calibration at 470HP: dmg9 → naive cush 0.39; dmg10 → cush 0.31, weak-random 8/8 DEATHS; dmg11 → naive cush 0.24, weak-random 8/8 deaths, optimized WR 1.0. dmg14+570HP overshoots (kills even naive and optimized).
corrected_statement: doom_knight is correctly winnable but its 6 base damage is far too low to impose a real cost — it never converts a marginal deck into a loss and leaves a fat 0.66 cushion. The intended 'easy but you pay HP' is achieved at ~470HP / 10dmg: naive wins at a tense 0.31 cushion, a genuinely weak/random deck DIES (the desired mid-game failure of random), and an optimized deck still clears (WR 1.0). HP alone does little; damage is the lever.
proposed_fix: C1: raise doom_knight baseHP 380→460-470 and baseDamage 6→10 (keep cd 2.5s, keep enrage@0.5). This makes random fail by loop 10, pressures naive to ~0.3 cushion, and keeps optimized comfortable. Do NOT push damage past ~11 at this HP or it begins killing even prepared decks.

target: iron_golem shield behavior (loop 20, shield 4 every 8s) — and shield bosses generally (desert_golem 10/7s, boss_iron_golem 12/7s)
claim: Shield/drain behaviors are near-inert; multi_hit is the only real threat.
method: Direct behavior-isolation enemyOverride A/B (boss-gradient-results.json, boss-shield-results.json): same naive deck vs each shield boss with the shield present, with affinity/behaviors removed, and with shield amount scaled up, 6 repeats.
evidence: iron_golem shield is LITERALLY inert: base (shield 4/8s) vs affinity-null → IDENTICAL WR 1.0 / cushion 0.81 / ttk 22s / dmg 444. Only at 5x (shield 20/4s) did it bite (naive DIED, 6/6). BUT bigger shields are NOT inert as a DPS tax: desert_golem shield 10/7s removed cut ttk 32s→18s and raised cushion 0.78→0.89; boss_iron_golem shield 12/7s removed cut ttk 38s→27s and cushion 0.50→0.65. Even an extreme 40/4s shield on boss_iron_golem still produced WR 1.0 (cushion 0.40) — no loss. So shields tax time/cushion proportionally to amount but never outrace player DPS into a kill.
corrected_statement: PARTIALLY CONFIRMED. iron_golem's 4-armor/8s shield is fully inert (zero measurable effect — it is below per-hit damage so it is always absorbed and never compounds). Larger shields (desert/ancient golem) are NOT inert: they meaningfully tax DPS and cushion. But NO shield value tested ever causes a loss — shields are a soft DPS-tax knob, not a threat. The 'near-inert' label is exactly right for iron_golem and directionally right for the others (inert as a danger, mild as a tax).
proposed_fix: C1: fix iron_golem's degenerate 4-shield — either raise shieldAmount to ~14-18 and shorten interval to ~5000ms (so it becomes a real DPS tax like the other golems) OR replace the shield with a behavior that can actually threaten (e.g. periodic small multi_hit). Shield should remain a 'tax that buys the boss time', so pair any shield buff with a damage/multi_hit buff that supplies the actual lethality.

target: bog_witch drain behavior (loop 40, drain healPercent 15) + multi_hit
claim: Drain behaviors are near-inert; multi_hit is the only real threat.
method: Behavior-isolation enemyOverride A/B (boss-gradient-results.json, boss-gradient2-results.json): naive deck vs bog_witch with drain at 15%, drain tripled to 45%, drain removed, and multi_hit isolated/scaled, 6 repeats.
evidence: Drain is inert: base (drain 15% + multi_hit 2x0.6) vs drain tripled to 45% → IDENTICAL WR 1.0 / cushion 0.90 / ttk 17s / dmg 480. Removing multi_hit (drain-only) barely moved cushion 0.90→0.94. By contrast multi_hit IS the lever: bog_witch multi_hit raised to 3x@1.0 crashed cushion 0.90→0.49 (same ttk, same WR). On infernal_dragon, removing multi_hit raised cushion 0.73→0.82. bog_witch is the safest boss in the game (100% WR / 0.909 cushion across the matrix).
corrected_statement: CONFIRMED. Drain at 15% (even tripled to 45%) is completely inert — the player out-races the heal so fast (17s ttk) the boss never recovers meaningful HP. multi_hit is the ONLY behavior that moves the needle: at damageMultiplier≈1.0 it roughly halves cushion. bog_witch's drain+weak-multi (2x0.6) combo leaves it the softest fight in the rotation despite sitting at loop 40.
proposed_fix: C1: drain is the wrong threat for an HP/DPS race — either drop it or make it gate-relevant (e.g. drain a flat large chunk on a long cooldown so it punishes slow decks). For bog_witch specifically, the real fix is its multi_hit: raise damageMultiplier 0.6→~0.9 and/or hitCount 2→3, and bump baseDamage 7→9, so loop-40 bog_witch stops being the safest fight in the game.

target: multi_hit behavior (lizard_king 2x0.6, bog_witch 2x0.6, infernal_dragon 3x0.5, boss_iron_golem none)
claim: multi_hit is the only behavior that constitutes a real threat.
method: Cross-boss behavior isolation: compared anyLoss/cushion of multi_hit bosses vs non-multi_hit bosses in boss-report.json, plus direct multi_hit-removed and multi_hit-scaled enemyOverride A/B on bog_witch and infernal_dragon (boss-gradient2-results.json).
evidence: infernal_dragon (multi_hit 3x0.5) is the ONLY boss with a double-digit loss rate (10.5% anyLoss, lowest cushion 0.607). Removing its multi_hit raised naive cushion 0.73→0.82. Scaling bog_witch multi_hit to 3x1.0 crashed cushion 0.90→0.49 — the single largest behavior swing measured. Shield (iron_golem) and drain (bog_witch) A/Bs produced ~0 change. enrage fires too late/too rarely to matter (it is paired on every soft boss yet none of them lose). multi_hit is the only behavior whose isolation changes outcomes.
corrected_statement: CONFIRMED. multi_hit (especially hitCount≥3 at damageMultiplier≥~0.8) is the only boss behavior that materially raises threat — it is the reason infernal_dragon is the hardest boss. shield = mild DPS tax (no losses), drain = inert, enrage = mostly cosmetic at current thresholds. The boss design lever that works is multi_hit.
proposed_fix: C1: standardize multi_hit as the primary boss-threat lever from loop 40 on. Give every boss from bog_witch onward a multi_hit of hitCount 2-3 at damageMultiplier 0.7-0.9 (currently bog_witch 0.6, lizard_king 0.6 are too weak; boss_iron_golem has none). Pair with the per-boss baseDamage bumps above. Keep total expected damage tuned so optimized decks clear with cushion >~0.5 while naive is pressured to ~0.3 and random fails.

target: archetype: pure burn / burn bank-and-cash + detonators (mage, fire)
claim: Prior pass flagged 'pure-burn' as weak imprecisely (whole-deck attribution). Re-examine: is optimized burn fine and only naive/random burn weak?
method: Faithful reconstruction A/B at identical boss1 profile (runXP 520 and 560, 8 repeats) of the EXACT curated optimized burn deck vs doom_knight, alongside curated optimized poison / poison-burn / detonator-control on the same hero. Reproduced val numbers exactly (burn-O cushion 0.134, poison-O 0.803, poisonburn-O 0.567, detcontrol-O 0.591 at runXP560), confirming the reconstruction and the underlying val data.
evidence: Optimized burn vs doom_knight: winRate 1.0 but cushion ONLY 0.091 (runXP520) / 0.134 (runXP560), ttk 24,100ms (24s to kill a 380-HP boss that 'should be easy'). The naive burn deck mage-pure-burn-boss1-2 is one of only TWO outright LOSERS vs doom_knight in the entire 78-deck boss1 set (winRate 0, 4 deaths; boss-report.json doomKnightCheck.losers). Pure_burn archetype rollup: bossWR 0.875 with 4 deaths. By contrast at the SAME hero: pure poison cushion 0.793 / ttk 10,000ms; poison-burn-hybrid 0.545; detonator-control 0.570. Burn is the weakest surviving damage archetype at the first boss across the catalog (archetype-summary boss cushion: pure-burn 0.231, pure_burn 0.368, burn 0.370, burn-bank+detonators 0.476 — the four lowest non-dedeath rows).
corrected_statement: Burn is NOT merely 'naive-weak / optimized-fine'. It is structurally thin against the first boss at BOTH quality levels: optimized burn barely survives doom_knight at 0.13 cushion via a 24-second grind, and a representative naive burn deck outright dies. The root cause is the burn ceiling: with 6-stack cap + soft-cap per-tick (8+floor((n-8)/2)) the banked pool is small, so detonators (3 Pierce/burn consumed) cash out for too little, and burn's ttk is far slower than poison/hybrid against a high-attack boss. Poison-burn-hybrid (0.55) and detonator-control (0.57) are healthy because poison closes faster; PURE burn is the off-curve archetype.
proposed_fix: C5/C1-compliant ceiling buff (NOT cost/CD): raise the burn-detonator per-burn payoff. A/B tested raising the 'deal N per [burn] consumed' value from 3 to 5 on t3-attack-fire-fire and t2-fire-fire: cushion 0.134 -> 0.370 and ttk 24,100ms -> 18,700ms vs doom_knight, while staying well below poison's 0.80 (no over-correction / not oppressive). Bumping to 7/6 added raw damage but did not improve cushion/ttk further (kill-point saturates), so +2 (3->5) is the minimal sufficient buff. This lifts burn's build-around ceiling per the Tier charter without touching cost or cooldown. (A CD cut on t1-fire/t2-fire-fire alone only reached 0.213 — less effective; the detonator payoff is the correct lever.)

target: archetype: air/earth slow+stun control + physical (warrior)
claim: Is this archetype weak only when naive (fine), or weak even when optimized (real problem)?
method: Direct boss2 A/B at identical profile (runXP 1150, loopMult 1.05, 8 repeats): the curated slow-stun-control N and O decks vs iron_golem, alongside the curated pure-physical / bleed-rage / mixed-bruiser NAIVE (N) decks as peer controls on the same hero.
evidence: slow-stun-control NAIVE: 8 deaths, winRate 0 vs iron_golem. slow-stun-control OPTIMIZED: wins but cushion 0.568 via the SLOWEST ttk of all (30,900ms). The PEER NAIVE decks all win comfortably: pure-physical-N cushion 0.541/ttk 20,100ms, bleed-rage-N 0.589/21,200ms, mixed-bruiser-N 0.681/18,000ms. In the full val set this archetype is the only NAMED archetype appearing in the worst-5 losers for iron_golem, desert_golem AND boss_iron_golem (always the N variant dying); archetype rollup bossWR 0.7, 12 deaths — the worst warrior archetype at both quality levels.
corrected_statement: The warrior 'slow+stun control + physical' archetype is a REAL viability problem, not just a quality artifact. Even other archetypes' NAIVE decks beat iron_golem, yet this archetype's naive deck dies and its optimized deck only scrapes by with a 31-second grind. Per C5 slow/stun are pure control with NO damage, so the deck pays too much win-condition tax against iron_golem's defense-affinity armor ratchet; it lacks the direct damage to break the ratchet before attrition kills it.
proposed_fix: Do NOT add damage to slow/stun (C5 locks them as pure control) and do NOT nerf the boss. Per C3, control archetypes are intentionally race-losing and must lean on a SECOND win condition: the archetype's physical payoff cards need a snappier curve so the 'physical' half can actually close. C1-compliant: cut cooldown on the archetype's primary attack finishers (e.g. t3-air-attack-* / t3-attack-counter-earth line) toward the ~1.2-1.6s floor so optimized ttk drops from ~31s toward the ~18-21s band the peer warrior archetypes hit; and/or buff the armor->damage finisher hook so a control player can convert their bought time. Target: optimized cushion >=0.6 and naive winRate 1.0 vs iron_golem.

target: archetype: mage slow-stun-control (e.g. mage-slow-stun-control-boss1-O)
claim: Mage pure-control viability vs the first boss (doom_knight).
method: Identical-profile reconstruction vs doom_knight at runXP 520 and 560, 8 repeats (reproduces val 0.016 cushion exactly).
evidence: At runXP 560 the curated optimized mage slow-stun-control deck wins but cushion 0.016 with ttk 57,800ms (57.8 SECONDS to kill doom_knight). At runXP 520 (one level lower) the same deck DIES (8 deaths, winRate 0 in my run). archetype-summary mage slow-stun-control boss1: cushion 0.319, with the O variant at 0.016.
corrected_statement: Mage pure slow-stun-control is on a knife-edge vs the first boss — it technically passes in the val data (winRate 1, cushion 0.016) but only by grinding for ~58s, and it flips to a loss with a single level of variance. Like the warrior control archetype, it lacks a closing win condition. It is borderline rather than outright broken (the curated mixed/detonator control decks are fine at 0.54-0.57), so this is the SAME structural issue as the warrior control finding, scoped to pure-control mage shells specifically.
proposed_fix: Same lever as the warrior control finding (C1/C3): pure-control shells need an accessible secondary damage outlet with a snappy CD so they can convert bought time into a kill faster than ~58s. Either ensure control decks reliably include a low-CD elemental closer, or cut CD on the control archetype's attached damage finishers. Do not buff slow/stun damage (C5 locked). Target optimized cushion >=0.4 and no level-1 flip to a loss vs doom_knight.

target: all warrior physical/bruiser/armor archetypes + mage poison/poison-block/heal/detonator/mixed-elemental
claim: Which archetypes are fine (clear stage battery + boss at a realistic profile)?
method: Read archetype-summary.json rollups (boss winRate/cushion/deaths) + identical-profile A/B spot checks vs doom_knight (8 repeats) and iron_golem (8 repeats).
evidence: All non-control physical archetypes are healthy: armor-finisher boss cushion 0.931 (1.0 normals, 0 deaths), bleed_rage 0.65, mixed-bruiser 0.646, pure-physical 0.55, mixed physical+splash 0.609 — all winRate 1, 0 deaths. Mage non-burn-non-control: poison-block/defensive-poison 0.999-1.0, pure-poison 0.849, heal-sustain 0.863-0.93, mixed-elemental-toolbox 0.656, poison-burn-hybrid 0.727, detonator-control 0.711 — all winRate ~1. My A/B confirmed peer naive warrior decks (phys/bleed/mixed) all beat iron_golem at 0.54-0.68 cushion, and optimized poison hit 0.79-0.80 vs doom_knight.
corrected_statement: The large majority of archetypes are viable and well-tuned at both quality levels: warrior physical/bleed/armor/bruiser lines and mage poison/poison-block/heal/detonator-control/mixed-elemental/poison-burn-hybrid all clear their stage boss with winRate ~1 and comfortable cushion. The only off-curve-WEAK archetypes are pure burn (structural) and pure slow+stun control (warrior, and borderline for mage). Armor/poison-block sit at near-1.0 cushion (intentionally elite at survival per C3) which is by-design, not over-strong.
proposed_fix: No change needed for these archetypes. Keep C3 defensive archetypes (armor-finisher, poison-block) at their high cushion — they are race-losing-but-durable by design, not oppressive (they do not also out-damage). Focus fixes on the two weak archetypes above.

target: quality-matrix.json: optimized vs naive boss cushion
claim: Optimized decks should be safer than naive/random at bosses.
method: Read quality-matrix.json boss cushion by quality tier across loop bands.
evidence: Optimized boss cushion is LOWER than naive in multiple bands: 1-10 optimized 0.551 vs naive 0.597 vs random 0.648; 11-20 optimized 0.696 vs naive 0.816; 21-40 optimized 0.717 vs naive 0.796; 61-70 optimized 0.687 vs naive 0.786. Optimized winRate is >= naive everywhere (1.0) and pctNoCycle is much higher for optimized (e.g. 0.573 vs 0.359 at 1-10).
corrected_statement: There is a real, mild inversion: 'optimized' build-around decks finish bosses with LOWER HP cushion than naive decks, because they pack more late-coming build-around cards (higher pctNoCycle = more of the deck never cycles in) and trade early safety for a higher assembled ceiling. This is consistent with the Tier charter (T3 edge is ceiling-when-assembled, not early floor) and is not a defect, but it does mean win-rate-saturated boss data understates how thin optimized decks run — reinforcing why cushion/ttk (not winRate) is the right metric and why the burn/control thinness is a true signal, not noise.
proposed_fix: No direct balance change. Methodology note for the synthesizer: grade archetypes on cushion + ttk, never winRate alone (winRate is 1.0 for nearly all archetypes incl. the weak ones). The optimized<naive cushion inversion confirms boss winRate is saturated and uninformative.
